summary:
The New Kingdom of Egypt, spanning from approximately 1550 to 1069 BC, represented the height of ancient Egyptian imperial power, artistic achievement, and international influence. Founded when the Theban pharaoh Ahmose I expelled the [[event:1799|Hyksos]] invaders and reunified Egypt, the New Kingdom saw the country transformed into a major military empire that extended Egyptian control deep into Nubia and the Levant.

The New Kingdom is defined by a succession of remarkable rulers. [[event:1801|Hatshepsut]] served as one of Egypt's few female pharaohs, presiding over a prosperous reign marked by ambitious construction projects and a famous trading expedition to the land of Punt. [[event:1802|Thutmose III]] extended Egyptian power to its greatest territorial reach through seventeen military campaigns, winning the decisive [[event:1803|Battle of Megiddo]] against a Canaanite coalition. Later, [[event:1807|Ramesses II]] dominated the era through his long reign, celebrated building program, and the famous contest with the Hittites at the [[event:1808|Battle of Kadesh]].

The [[event:1804|Amarna Period]] under [[event:1805|Akhenaten]] represented a radical theological and artistic interlude. The pharaoh dismantled the traditional polytheistic religion in favor of Aten, the sun disk, moving the capital to a new city at Amarna and overseeing a distinctive naturalistic art style. His successor [[event:1806|Tutankhamun]] reversed these reforms, restoring the traditional cults and the primacy of Amun. The Amarna episode left lasting disruption in Egyptian religious institutions.

New Kingdom Egypt was a major player in the international diplomatic system of the Late Bronze Age, exchanging embassies, gifts, and princesses with the Hittites, Mitanni, Babylon, and Assyria. The [[event:1811|Valley of the Kings]] near Thebes served as the royal necropolis, where pharaohs were buried in rock-cut tombs stocked with elaborate funerary equipment. Egyptian temples of the period, including the [[event:1885|Karnak complex]] and [[event:1810|Abu Simbel]], remain among the most impressive architectural achievements of antiquity.

The New Kingdom declined during the Twentieth Dynasty under pressure from the [[event:1814|Sea Peoples]], internal power struggles between pharaonic authority and the increasingly powerful priesthood of Amun, and economic contraction. By around 1069 BC the state had fragmented into the [[event:1813|Third Intermediate Period]], with rival dynasties ruling Upper and Lower Egypt simultaneously.
